summary: The paper makes two headline claims: (1) OMP oracle 'decisively falsifies' the amortization gap hypothesis, and (2) encoder weight norm (AUROC=0.757) is a novel absorption detector that outperforms EDA. Both claims have serious experimental and logical flaws. The OMP experiment operates at near-100% ceiling with only 3 letters x 30 tokens and is labeled PILOT throughout — there is zero statistical power to detect a difference, making the 'decisive falsification' language scientifically indefensible. The AUROC=0.757 detection result rests on n_pos=18 labeled features, all from a single model (GPT-2 Small, 117M) and a single layer (L6), with the encoder norm signal inverting direction at L2, L8, and L10. The 'cross-architecture replication' (AUROC=0.837 on TopK-32k) uses proxy labels that E1 confirms are not actual absorbed features — the TopK-32k SAE shows 0% absorption under IG measurement. The cross-hierarchy contribution (Contribution 3 from the proposal) is entirely absent, replaced by a width recovery analysis with a fatal hook-confound. Four submission blockers remain unresolved. The paper has real positive elements: honest negative-result reporting, pre-committed falsification criteria, and the O_jaccard signal as an independent complement. But these cannot compensate for the fundamental statistical power problems and the overgeneralized mechanistic framing.

findings.description: The OMP experiment's ceiling effect makes the null result experimentally meaningless. Feedforward AR=0.978 and OMP AR=0.978 across 3 letters x 30 tokens each. Every experiment file in exp/results/full/ is labeled mode='PILOT'; the C2 file explicitly notes 'C1 pilot found ceiling effect'. With baseline absorption rate of 0.978 and n=30 tokens per letter, the standard error on the proportion difference is sqrt(2 * 0.978 * (1-0.978) / 30) = 0.037. The minimum detectable 5pp difference (p<0.05, two-tailed) requires n >= 317 tokens per letter. The pre-committed criterion of 'OMP >= 80% of FF absorption rate' is satisfied trivially at 1.000 regardless of any real effect, because the baseline is already 97.8%. The paper claims 'the result is unambiguous' and 'decisively falsifies' but reports zero confidence intervals on the null result and no power analysis. This is a critical methodological failure: a null result at near-zero power cannot constitute a falsification.
findings.suggestion: Re-run the experiment with at least 500-1000 tokens per letter (based on power analysis: to detect 5pp reduction at 80% power with baseline AR=0.978, need n>=317) and all 26 letters. Report bootstrap 95% CI on (AR_OMP - AR_FF). Downgrade 'decisively falsifies' to 'is consistent with'. Test at a layer with lower baseline AR (L2 ratio=0.877 from A2 data) where there is headroom to detect differences.

findings.description: All core experiments are in PILOT mode with drastically smaller scale than methodology specifies. A1 (PILOT), A2 (PILOT), A3 (PILOT), B2 (PILOT), C2 (PILOT), D2 (PILOT) — confirmed by the mode field in all result JSONs. The C2 experiment was supposed to use 10,000 tokens and 26 letters (methodology, Block C2); it used 90 tokens and 3 letters. The B1 co-occurrence graph used 10,000 tokens, not 200,000 as proposed. The D2 cross-hierarchy experiment used 100 tokens per category, not 200. The paper presents pilot-scale data as final published results without acknowledging this gap from the experimental plan.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) re-run all experiments at the specified scale and present those as the final results, or (b) explicitly label all results as pilot-scale and add a limitations section acknowledging that full-scale validation has not been completed. Option (a) is required for submission.

findings.description: The cross-architecture 'replication' (AUROC=0.837 on TopK-32k) uses proxy labels that are demonstrably not absorbed features. E1 results show absorption_rate=0.0 for letters a, e, o, s, t in the TopK-32k SAE under IG measurement. The n_pos=77 labels come from decoder-alignment (cosine >= 0.30 to letter probes), not from the absorption detection pipeline. These 77 features are decoder-directions that happen to align with letter probes — a completely different quantity. The paper reports this as 'cross-architecture replication' and uses AUROC=0.837 as the headline result in the abstract, but the labels are not absorption labels. The actual absorption detection capability on TopK-32k is not established because no absorbed features were found (or the measurement failed, which is equally problematic).
findings.suggestion: Reframe the AUROC=0.837 result explicitly as 'EncNorm predicts decoder-letter-probe alignment' not 'EncNorm detects absorbed features in TopK architecture.' Either generate proper IG-based absorption labels for the TopK-32k SAE (investigating why absorption_rate=0.0, possibly by testing more letters or adjusting thresholds), or remove the cross-architecture AUROC comparison from the main results.

findings.description: The paper claims encoder norm is theoretically motivated by the sparsity landscape account but the stated mechanism contradicts the Tang et al. theory. Section 5.1 argues: 'when child c fires on token t in place of parent j, the reconstruction residual is attributed to j's encoder direction; the encoder gradient pushes ||W_enc,j||_2 upward.' But Tang et al. describe absorption as a stable partial minimum where the encoder direction of j drifts AWAY from its correct direction under persistent suppressive gradient from c. The suppressive gradient from c reduces the encoder's ability to fire on j-relevant tokens — this suppression should decrease the effective encoder norm contribution or shift its direction, not inflate it. The A2 data is consistent with this ambiguity: absorbed features have LOWER encoder norms than non-absorbed features at L2 (ratio=0.877), L8 (0.891), and L10 (0.933). The mechanism is layer-specific and the theoretical story does not explain why it inverts at most layers. The AUROC=0.757 is a real empirical correlation; the mechanistic narrative is post-hoc speculation that is not well-supported.
findings.suggestion: Either provide a formal gradient-level derivation showing that gradient competition specifically inflates (not deflates) encoder norms at mid-layers, or reduce Section 3.1 to describe EncNorm as 'an empirically discovered heuristic whose mechanistic basis is unclear.' The empirical result stands; the mechanistic narrative should not be presented as established theory.

findings.description: The cross-hierarchy contribution (H3/Contribution 3 from the proposal) is entirely absent. D2 shows entity-type absorption rate = 0.000 with negative control = 0.050 — the direction is INVERTED relative to H3's prediction. The paper correctly attributes this to probe quality issues, but the D2 notes reveal a deeper methodological flaw: 'Used entity-type probe on control sentences for comparison.' The negative control absorption rate was measured using the entity-type probe, not a probe trained on the control concept. This means the 5% negative control absorption rate is an artifact of applying the wrong probe. The cross-hierarchy experiment is invalid on multiple levels: wrong probe for control, pilot scale (100 tokens), and 0% absorption in the positive hierarchy.
findings.suggestion: Remove all cross-hierarchy generalization claims from the contribution list. The width recovery analysis (67% recovery with hook-confound caveat) is the actual third empirical contribution. Restate contributions as: (1) Mechanistic: OMP oracle result, (2) Methodological: EncNorm + O_jaccard, (3) Practical: width recovery analysis.

findings.description: The width recovery analysis (F1: 67% of absorbed features recovered in wider SAE) is confounded by different hook points in the two SAEs. Standard-24k uses blocks.6.hook_resid_pre (before attention at L6); TopK-32k uses blocks.6.hook_resid_post (after attention at L6). The two SAEs operate on different activation spaces. Comparing decoder directions trained on different activation spaces via cosine similarity does not measure semantic recovery — it measures coincidental geometric alignment between weight matrices trained on different input distributions. The paper flags this as an 'upper bound' but then makes claims ('67% of absorbed features have geometric counterparts') that cannot be supported without quantifying how much of the similarity is explained by the hook-point shift. The attention sublayer at L6 in GPT-2 Small transforms the residual stream significantly.
findings.suggestion: Compute the average cosine similarity between GPT-2 L6 resid_pre and resid_post activations on a sample of tokens. This measures the representational shift caused by the attention sublayer, providing an empirical baseline for 'expected cosine similarity due to hook confound.' The 67% figure is currently uninterpretable without this control.

findings.description: The encoder norm signal is layer-specific in a way that invalidates the generalization claim. A2 results: ratio (absorbed/non-absorbed encoder norm) = 0.877 at L2, 1.055 at L4, 1.267 at L6, 0.891 at L8, 0.933 at L10. The signal is only in the predicted direction at L4 and L6; at L2, L8, L10 it inverts (absorbed features have LOWER encoder norms). The paper reports 'EncNorm peaks at L6' as a positive finding but does not address the inversion at other layers. A practitioner applying EncNorm to an L8 or L10 SAE would get an absorption detector that predicts the WRONG direction. The Mann-Whitney test for early vs. late absorbed features (A2: p=0.055) is borderline non-significant with n=10 vs. n=8 — no reliable early/late distinction is established.
findings.suggestion: The EncNorm signal should be presented as 'L6-specific on GPT-2 Small' not as a general absorption detector. Add a prominent limitation noting that the direction of the effect reverses at other layers and architectures have not been tested. The AUROC=0.757 claim should be scoped to 'Standard/L1 SAE at GPT-2 L6.'

findings.description: EncNorm and EDA have Spearman r=0.712 across all 24,576 latents (A2, A1 results), yet the paper claims they 'provide independent information' and that the DeLong test improvement validates EncNorm as a distinct contribution. With r=0.712, approximately 51% of EncNorm's variance is shared with EDA. The AUROC improvement (0.757 vs. 0.650) could be entirely explained by EncNorm having a distribution shape that is more favorable for classification at this imbalance ratio (18/24576 = 0.073%), rather than capturing genuinely independent absorption-relevant information. The paper does not control for this distributional confound.
findings.suggestion: Run a partial analysis: fit a logistic regression with EDA as a predictor; then test whether adding EncNorm as a second predictor significantly improves AUROC (using a likelihood ratio test or DeLong test on the combined vs. single-predictor scores). If EncNorm AUROC after controlling for EDA is not significantly better than EDA alone, the marginal contribution of EncNorm is not established.

findings.description: The paper does not engage with MP-SAE (Costa et al., arXiv:2506.03093), which uses iterative matching pursuit during training (joint encoder+decoder optimization). The paper falsifies the 'weak amortization gap hypothesis' (inference-time OMP with fixed decoder cannot reduce absorption) but conflates this with falsifying the 'strong amortization gap hypothesis' (jointly trained iterative encoder+decoder cannot reduce absorption). If MP-SAE shows reduced absorption, the practical recommendation ('do not expect iterative encoders to help') would be incorrect — it should say 'do not expect post-hoc iterative encoders to help, but training with iterative encoders may.' The paper's Section 5.1 guidance is currently misleading.
findings.suggestion: Add a paragraph in Section 5.1 explicitly distinguishing (a) inference-time OMP with fixed decoder [tested here] from (b) joint training of iterative encoder+decoder [not tested here]. Cite Costa et al. MP-SAE and report their absorption reduction results. If MP-SAE reduces absorption, the paper's practical guidance requires qualification.

findings.description: All results are from GPT-2 Small (117M, 2019 vintage). Gemma Scope was inaccessible (401 Unauthorized, E1). The paper makes broad claims about 'SAE feature absorption' but has no results on any model released after 2019. NeurIPS main track requires generalization across at least 2-3 architectures. The Spearman r=0.712 between EncNorm and EDA (which is theoretically grounded) at least supports that EncNorm has some empirical validity, but the specific AUROC numbers, layer-specificity, and mechanistic story may not transfer to Gemma-2, Llama-3, or Claude SAEs.
findings.suggestion: Either gain access to Gemma Scope (accept HuggingFace gated license at huggingface.co/google/gemma-scope) and run the primary experiments on Gemma-2-2B, or prominently scope all claims to GPT-2 Small. The venue target (NeurIPS 2026 main track) is incompatible with single-model GPT-2 Small results given the current interpretability literature's focus on Gemma Scope and larger models.

findings.description: The Jaccard co-occurrence score (O_jaccard) has Precision@50=0.10 (5 true positives out of 50 flagged). While 136x above base rate, 90% of flagged features are false positives. The B2 notes reveal an important caveat: 9 of the 18 absorbed features are NOT in the letter feature set, so O_jaccard is 0 for these by construction (the max is taken only over features in the letter set). This means O_jaccard coverage is structurally limited to absorbed features that overlap with known letter features — it cannot detect the other half of absorbed features regardless of signal quality. This is a significant gap between the claimed 'independent signal' and the actual coverage.
findings.suggestion: Report O_jaccard coverage separately: (a) for the 9 absorbed features that ARE in the letter set (expected to have non-zero O_jaccard) and (b) for the 9 that are not. The AUROC of O_jaccard in the literature-set subset may be substantially higher than 0.721. The claim that O_jaccard is 'complementary' to EncNorm should be scoped to features with non-zero letter-set membership.

findings.description: The Jaccard score definition is inconsistent between the paper body and the experimental implementation. Section 3.2 states f_k > f_j (strictly greater activation frequency); the B2 results notes confirm f_i > 3*f_j was used in the actual experiment. The A_cooccur values being bounded at 0.333 (= 1/3 by construction when f_i > 3*f_j) is noted in the B2 JSON. The paper body should match the experimental implementation.
findings.suggestion: Update Section 3.2 to use f_k > 3*f_j consistently. This also explains the bound at 0.333 for A_cooccur values seen in the B2 data — the definition should be explicitly stated in the paper.

findings.description: All 6 required figures (fig_teaser.pdf, fig_enc_norm_hist.pdf, fig_omp_design.pdf, fig_roc_curves.pdf, fig_ablation.pdf, fig_omp_results.pdf) do not exist in writing/latex/figures/. The directory contains fig1_eda_method.pdf through fig5_phase_stability.pdf from a prior iteration. The LaTeX document has broken figure references and a reviewer reading paper.md cannot evaluate any visual claims. Tables 1-4 are present as inline markdown and are well-formatted.
findings.suggestion: Generate the 6 required figures from experiment result JSON files before any review submission. Use the academic-plotting skill for publication-quality output. This is a pre-submission blocker.

findings.description: The paper's notation.md specifies T=200,000 tokens for co-occurrence graph construction. The actual experiment used T=10,000 (confirmed in B2 JSON: n_tokens_for_cooccurrence=10000). With 24,576 features and 10,000 tokens, pairs of features with activation frequency < 0.01 (rare features) have expected co-activation count < 1. The majority of A_cooccur values of exactly 0.333 in the B2 data reflect features with a single shared activation — this is not a reliable co-occurrence estimate. The paper should correct the notation.md T value and add a caveat about sparse co-occurrence statistics.
findings.suggestion: Either re-run B1 with T=200,000 tokens as specified, or update notation.md to T=10,000 and add a note that with 24,576 features and 10,000 tokens, many co-occurrence statistics are estimated from single observations.

metric_gaming_risks: AUROC=0.757 at n_pos=18 has bootstrap CI spanning 0.184 AUROC units [0.665, 0.849]. With 18 positive examples, a single mislabeled feature shifts the AUROC by approximately 0.001 per threshold step — the estimate is fragile. The DeLong test's Type I error control at n_pos=18 is not validated.

The TopK-32k 'replication' AUROC=0.837 uses proxy labels (decoder-alignment cosine >= 0.30). E1 shows 0% actual absorption rate in TopK-32k under IG measurement. The AUROC measures decoder-letter alignment prediction, not absorption detection. Presenting this as a replication inflates confidence in the EncNorm finding.

OMP experiment: 97-100% absorption rate under both conditions provides near-zero power to detect any difference. The null result (0% reduction) is consistent with both 'amortization gap hypothesis falsified' and 'measurement too noisy to detect effect' — these cannot be distinguished at the current sample size.

O_jaccard AUROC=0.721 applies only to features with non-zero letter-set membership (9 of 18 absorbed features have O_jaccard=0 by construction because they are not in the letter feature set). The effective AUROC within the detectable subset may be substantially different.
Layer specificity: EncNorm signal direction inverts at L2, L8, L10 (ratio below 1.0). Reporting AUROC only for L6 cherry-picks the layer with favorable signal.
novelty_risks: Encoder weight norm as a feature 'health' indicator has implicit precedent in Anthropic's monosemanticity work and SAELens tooling. The paper needs to explicitly compare to prior uses of weight norm statistics in SAE analysis to establish novelty beyond 'absorbed features have different weight statistics.'

The OMP-as-oracle-encoder comparison is a standard technique in sparse coding (Olshausen & Field 1996; Mairal et al. 2010). The novelty claim should explicitly differentiate from prior work using pursuit algorithms as oracle baselines for sparse dictionary learning. The specific claim — testing whether OMP reduces absorption in pre-trained SAEs — may be novel as applied, but the methodology has decades of precedent.

Co-occurrence-based feature analysis has precedent in the Geometry of Concepts paper (arXiv:2410.19750) and in neuroscience-inspired feature analysis. The paper differentiates by targeting absorption specifically, but should cite co-occurrence approaches in the SAE literature and explain the differentiation more carefully.

The 'successive refinement' framing (Equitz-Cover) applied to SAE width analysis is a genuinely novel framing. However, the result (67% recovery at 0.80 cosine threshold) is confounded by the hook-point difference, making the novelty claim about the empirical finding premature.

The paper does not discuss whether the encoder norm finding has been independently discovered in other ongoing SAE research (e.g., whether Anthropic's internal SAE tools already flag high-norm encoder rows as absorption suspects). Given the proximity to active industrial research programs, this is a real prior-art risk.
reproducibility_gaps: All core experiments are in PILOT mode at 10-30x smaller scale than the methodology specifies. The full-scale experiments have not been run. A researcher attempting to reproduce 'Table 1' from the paper would need to run the experiments at the specified scale and may get different AUROC values.
Gemma Scope is inaccessible (401 Unauthorized). All results are GPT-2 Small (117M) only. Zero replication on any model released after 2019.

The IG-based absorption labels (n_pos=18) were generated in iter_001 with a specific version of sae_spelling. The specific library version is not pinned in any experiment file. Label reproducibility requires the exact sae_spelling version.

The OMP implementation details are underspecified: which OMP algorithm variant (classical greedy, batch, Cholesky-update), how the stopping criterion is handled at exactly K=53 vs. feedforward mean L0=55.7. The 2-step mismatch between K and L0 is noted in the pilot but not addressed in C2.
All 6 required figures are missing from the LaTeX compilation directory. Visual claims (e.g., absorbed vs. non-absorbed encoder norm distribution) cannot be evaluated without the figures.

The D2 cross-hierarchy experiment used the entity-type probe on the negative control sentences (methodological error acknowledged in D2 notes). The true negative control absorption rate under a correctly-trained control probe is unknown.

B1 co-occurrence graph was built on 10,000 tokens despite the proposal specifying 200,000. With 24,576 features and 10,000 tokens, most rare-feature co-occurrence statistics are based on single observations.
